unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 1 of your Year 8 English course at Ehel Academy. In this unit you will explore the powerful theme of languages and communication -- how people share ideas, how rumours spread, and how the truth can be lost or found depending on how carefully we listen and speak. You will read a compelling story set in Mogadishu, learn important grammar structures, build a rich vocabulary, and practise all four language skills: reading, listening, speaking, and writing.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 8 learner analysing language, communication and truth

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Using few words to say something clearly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.

The word concise belongs to our vocabulary group 1 vocabulary.
I can explain concise in my own words.
I can connect concise to this unit's texts.
I can use concise in a clear Grade 8 sentence.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - c - i - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'concise', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Languages and Communication.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Summarise the central ideas and important details in Languages and Communication texts.
Support inferences and comparisons with relevant text evidence.
Use Grade 8 vocabulary accurately in discussion and writing.
Apply the unit language focus in connected sentences and paragraphs.
Create a cohesive multi-paragraph response for a defined purpose and audience.
Present a sustained five- to six-minute argument and defend ideas under questioning.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Languages and Communication: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 1 of your Year 8 English course at Ehel Academy. In this unit you will explore the powerful theme of languages and communication -- how people share ideas, how rumours spread, and how the truth can be lost or found depending on how carefully we listen and speak. You will read a compelling story set in Mogadishu, learn important grammar structures, build a rich vocabulary, and practise all four language skills: reading, listening, speaking, and writing.
This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through the material at your own speed, with the support of your AI tutor available around the clock and your teacher sessions twice a week. Every section includes activities, tips, and prompts to help you deepen your understanding. By the end of this unit you will be a more confident, thoughtful communicator -- someone who questions what they hear, chooses words with care, and speaks with purpose.
The story at the heart of this unit, "Whispers Beyond the Gate," follows a group of students in Mogadishu who discover how quickly misinformation can spread through a community and what happens when people act on rumour rather than fact. As you study this story, you will encounter the vocabulary words from your word list used in real contexts, and you will see the grammar patterns you are learning woven naturally into the narrative.
Throughout this lesson hub you will find "Ask Your AI Tutor" prompts. These are suggestions for questions you can ask your AI tutor at any time. Think of the tutor as a patient, knowledgeable study partner who is always ready to explain, give examples, or quiz you. Do not be shy about using it -- the more you interact, the faster you will learn.
By the end of this unit, you should be able to:
Use the contrast conjunctions although, while, and whereas accurately in spoken and written English.
Recognise and use -ing forms (gerunds) as subjects and objects in sentences.
Form and use the present continuous tense to describe actions happening now or in progress.
Identify and use abstract nouns such as serenity, foresight, dissent, and zeal in appropriate contexts.
Convert direct speech into reported speech, adjusting tense, pronouns, and time expressions correctly.
Construct zero and first conditional sentences to express facts and future possibilities.
Use connectives (because, so that, although, however, therefore) to link ideas logically.
Understand and use multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) such as give in, carry out, and speak up.
Read a full-length story critically, identifying themes, character development, and moral lessons.
Expand your vocabulary by learning and practising 33 new words from the Unit 1 word list.
Write a structured paragraph or short essay that uses contrast conjunctions and abstract nouns.
Speak about social issues with clarity, supporting your opinions with evidence.
Apply critical thinking to evaluate information and distinguish fact from rumour.
Before you dive into the reading and grammar, take some time to preview the key vocabulary for this unit. These are words you will encounter repeatedly in the story, grammar exercises, and writing tasks. Familiarising yourself with them now will make everything that follows easier to understand.
Your Unit 1 vocabulary list contains 33 words organised into groups by part of speech. Here is a quick overview of the groups and some highlighted words from each:
Nouns: barricade, zeal, definition, foresight, defiance, dissent, serenity, revelation, longevity, squalor, embargo, quibble
These nouns include several abstract nouns -- words for ideas, feelings, and qualities you cannot physically touch. Words like zeal (strong enthusiasm), serenity (calmness and peace), and foresight (the ability to plan for the future) are especially important in this unit because they appear in both the story and the grammar lessons on abstract nouns.
Verbs: allot, accustom, assimilate, concede, dispel, cite
These verbs describe mental and social actions. For example, to assimilate means to absorb and understand new information, and to cite means to refer to a source of evidence. You will use these verbs in your writing and speaking tasks.

readings.title: Languages and Communication: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Adjectives: concise, temperate, plausible, rueful, pensive, impending, shrewd, gullible, spontaneous, conscientious, bizarre, pervasive, conventional
These adjectives will help you describe people, situations, and feelings with precision. Notice how some are positive (conscientious, shrewd), some are negative (gullible, bizarre), and some are neutral (conventional, temperate). Understanding these shades of meaning will make your descriptions richer and more accurate.
Read the definition in your Vocabulary file and make sure you understand it in your own words.
Read the example sentence to see how the word is used naturally.
Write your own sentence using the word. Try to connect it to your own life or the story.
Say the word aloud several times to get comfortable with the pronunciation.
Test yourself by covering the definition and trying to recall it from memory.
Use the word in conversation with classmates, family, or your AI tutor during the week.
This unit covers seven grammar topics. The primary focus is on contrast conjunctions -- although, while, and whereas -- which help you show differences and unexpected connections between ideas. You will also study five additional grammar areas that work together with these conjunctions to make your English more expressive and precise.
1. Although / While / Whereas
These three words are called contrast conjunctions because they connect two ideas that are different from each other or that seem surprising when placed together. Learning to use them well is one of the most important skills in this unit.
Although introduces a surprising or unexpected fact. It signals that what follows might seem to contradict the main idea, but both are true. For example: Although the morning was calm, something felt strange. Here, you would expect a calm morning to feel normal, but the writer tells you it did not.
While can show contrast between two things that happen at the same time or that are true at the same time. For example: While Ibrahim preferred careful thinking, Nadia was ready to act immediately. Both things are true at once, and the writer wants you to notice the difference.
Whereas is more formal and is used specifically to highlight a direct comparison between two different things. For example: Cats are independent, whereas dogs are loyal. The structure draws a clear line between the two subjects.
A key point to remember: when although or while comes at the beginning of a sentence, you need a comma before the main clause. When whereas joins two clauses, it usually appears in the middle of the sentence with a comma before it.
2. -ing Forms as Subjects and Objects
You already know the -ing form from the present continuous tense (I am running). But -ing forms can also work as nouns -- they can be the subject or the object of a sentence. When used this way, they are called gerunds.
As a subject: Reading is a wonderful habit. Here, "reading" is the thing you are talking about. It acts like a noun.
As an object: I enjoy swimming. Here, "swimming" is the thing you enjoy. It comes after the verb and acts as the object.
This pattern is very common in English and appears throughout the unit story. Recognising gerunds will help you understand complex sentences and use them in your own writing.
The present continuous tense describes actions that are happening right now or are in progress. It is formed with the verb to be (am, is, are) plus the -ing form of the main verb.
Structure: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing
Examples: She is studying for her exam. They are discussing the news. I am writing an essay.
In the story, you will see present continuous used to bring scenes to life: students are whispering in the corridors, people are shouting at the protest, and the forum members are learning to think critically. Notice how this tense creates a feeling of immediacy -- you can almost see the action unfolding.

readings.title: Year 8 - Unit 1 Story, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Genre: Teen school mystery / social drama
Theme: Misinformation, student voice, truth vs. rumour
Setting: Present-day Mogadishu, Somalia (urban modern high school)
Moral: Truth needs no volume -- just courage, care, and questioning minds.
Look for vocabulary words from your Unit 1 list as you read. They appear naturally throughout the story.
Ibrahim walked to Musa Academy just after sunrise. Mogadishu shimmered in the early light -- the distant Indian Ocean sparkled beyond the rooftops, and tuk-tuks hummed past him as the smell of spiced shaah drifted from a corner cafe. It was a temperate morning, mild and pleasant, but something in the air felt strange, like a word caught between the teeth and never quite spoken.
At the school gate, his classmate Nadia waved him over. Her usually bright expression was clouded. She dropped her voice to a whisper.
"Have you heard about the embargo?" she asked, eyes wide. "The port is being shut down. No more rice, no fuel, no sugar. People are saying it is going to get very bad very quickly."
Ibrahim blinked. "What? There is nothing about that on the news."
"My cousin Musa told me," Nadia explained. "He says he knows someone in customs. He sent a flyer. Look." She pressed a crumpled paper into his hand.
Ibrahim read the bold red letters aloud: "STAND UP FOR SOMALIA. 3:30 PM. Bakaaraha Road. Bring signs. Block the trucks. Show your defiance."
There was no organiser's name. No logo. No source. Just urgent words in angry ink. His gut twisted with doubt.
"And we believe this?" he asked.
Amal bit her lip. "Musa sounded serious. And a protest is happening whether we go or not."
By mid-morning, the corridors of Musa Academy were buzzing with whispered conversations. Some students scrolled through TikTok, watching shaky clips that showed young people tipping crates across a road. The caption read: "We are blocking imports until the leaders hear us. Mogadishu, stand up!"
The rumour was becoming pervasive -- spreading through every classroom, every group chat, every whispered exchange under the stairwell. It seemed as though the entire school believed it.
Ibrahim and Nadia sat under the stairwell during break, watching the chaos grow on their screens.
"Is it real?" she asked. "Some of it looks staged."
"It could be from anywhere," Ibrahim said carefully. "We do not even know when the video was made. The story seems plausible, but plausible is not the same as true."
"Still," Nadia murmured, "if we do nothing, are we just sheep?"
Ibrahim hesitated. "Acting without facts is worse. If we join a lie, we are not helping -- we are hurting."
While Ibrahim was cautious and thoughtful, Nadia felt the pull of action. This difference between them would matter as the day unfolded.
In their history class, Mr. Ishmael -- a tall, quietly commanding man who had been teaching at Musa Academy for fifteen years -- introduced the vocabulary word foresight.
"Foresight is your ability to see what could happen before it does," he explained, writing the word on the board in careful letters. "It is not about being scared. It is about being wise. A person with foresight asks questions before acting."
A student named Samira raised her hand. "Sir, is not protest a way to stop something bad before it gets worse?"
"Yes," said Mr. Ishmael, "but protest only works when the reason is real and the facts are solid. A conscientious person checks first and acts second."
He turned back to the board. "Let us list examples of productive resistance -- movements that made a real difference -- and then failed ones. Think critically. Who had facts? Who just followed? I will allot ten minutes for group discussion."
Ibrahim wrote in his notebook: "If we do not think for ourselves, someone else will think for us." He underlined it twice.

readings.title: Year 8 - Unit 1 Story, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
At 3:30 PM, Ibrahim and Nadia walked to Bakaaraha Road. They told themselves they were only going to observe, not to participate. But the scene that greeted them was larger and louder than either of them had expected.
Crowds had gathered -- mostly teenagers, some filming on their phones, a few adults watching warily from behind market stalls. Several boys had built a barricade out of fruit crates and old tyres, blocking the road entirely.
"Do not let the trucks pass!" shouted one boy, his voice raw with zeal. "We will not give in!"
Another student with a portable speaker was yelling slogans: "They want us silent! We refuse to be silent!" A few people cheered. Others looked unsure, glancing around as if waiting for someone to tell them what to do.
Ibrahim scanned the crowd with shrewd eyes. Something felt off.
"Look," he pointed. "That boy -- the one with the speaker. He is filming himself, not the protest. He is laughing."
The boy took a selfie in front of the barricade. Another student tagged a post online: "Fake riot for the feed. #content"
"This is a joke," Ibrahim said, jaw tight with frustration. "These people do not care about truth. They want views and followers."
Amal shook her head. "We need to leave. Now."
Although the protest was loud and energetic, it was built on nothing real -- and they both knew it.
Back at school the next day, the atmosphere had shifted. Where there had been excitement and zeal, there was now embarrassment and silence. A local news outlet had published the truth:
"There is no official embargo. A viral student protest has been traced to a hoax flyer. Students were misled."
Mr. Ishmael stood before the class, disappointment etched into his face. The room was so quiet you could hear the ceiling fan turning.
"Who wrote the flyer?" he asked. No one answered.
He sighed. "Let me ask another question: what happens when we follow anger instead of truth?"
One boy muttered, "We look stupid."
Ibrahim raised his hand slowly. "We lose trust. People stop believing us -- even when we are telling the truth."
"Exactly," Mr. Ishmael said. "This is not about who started it. It is about what we choose to do next. The revelation that it was a hoax is painful, but it is also an opportunity."
That evening, Nadia received a voice message from her cousin Musa -- the same person who had told her about the protest in the first place.
His voice was shaky: "Nadia... I made the flyer. I just wanted to boost my follower count. I thought it would go viral and then everyone would forget. I did not know it would get this serious. I am sorry."
Amal sat frozen, staring at her phone. Then she whispered, "He started all of it."
Ibrahim clenched his fist. "People could have been hurt. There were younger students at that barricade. What if things had turned violent?"
"He was gullible enough to think there would be no consequences," Nadia said, her voice edged with dissent from Musa's carelessness. "And we were almost gullible enough to follow him blindly."
"We need to fix this," Ibrahim said. "Somehow."
Amal looked at him, pensive and thoughtful. "I think I know how."
The next morning, Ibrahim and Nadia pitched an idea to Mr. Ishmael: The Mogadishu Student Forum -- a weekly meeting where students could discuss current events, fact-check news, and debate real issues in a respectful environment.
"We want to allot time each week for open dialogue," Ibrahim explained. "A space where people can talk instead of shout, ask questions instead of follow blindly."
Mr. Ishmael studied them for a long moment. Then he nodded. "You are trying to be part of the solution instead of the problem. I will support this."
At the first meeting, forty students showed up. Some were quiet and nervous. Some were still angry about what had happened. But all of them were curious.

readings.title: Year 8 - Unit 1 Story, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"Whereas last week we followed a lie," Ibrahim began, standing in front of the group, "this week, we ask questions. That is how real change begins -- not with shouting, but with thinking."
Amal stepped forward. "We need to stop being so gullible. If you cannot cite your facts, do not post them. Do not act on them. Do not share them."
A Form 3 student named Ishmael added quietly, "Some of us joined the protest out of boredom. We did not think. I feel rueful about that -- genuinely sorry and a little ashamed."
Another student, Nadia, said: "The pressure to act is real. But silence is not always cowardice. Sometimes it is serenity -- choosing to wait, to learn, to understand before you move."
Part 8: From Mistake to Movement
In the weeks that followed, the forum became a safe space for honest discussion. Students learned to assimilate information carefully rather than react to it emotionally. They practised weighing evidence, questioning sources, and respecting viewpoints different from their own.
One week, they debated censorship. Another week, they discussed corruption. Then they tackled online disinformation -- the very thing that had started all of this.
Ibrahim moderated a fierce debate about whether protests should be spontaneous or carefully planned.
"Spontaneous actions can change the world," argued Samira. "History is full of moments where people rose up without warning."
"That is true," Nadia replied, "but conscientious actions -- ones where people think carefully and prepare -- leave fewer scars. They are slower, yes, but they tend to last. They have longevity."
A younger student read aloud from a note she had written: "If we do not think for ourselves, someone else will think for us."
The next day, that line appeared on the wall outside the school -- in permanent marker. No one knew who had written it, but no one erased it either.
Two months later, a new flyer appeared at the school gates. This one read: "Rise Up. Speak Loud. Ban school fees for all!"
Some students felt the familiar rush of excitement. Others felt a twinge of recognition -- they had seen this before.
Ibrahim picked up the paper and examined it calmly. He did not rush. He did not react. He smiled.
"All right," he said. "Let us check the source. Let us ask: Who wrote this? Who benefits? What is the data? Is this plausible, or is it another bizarre attempt to manipulate us?"
Instead of rushing into the streets, thirty students joined that week's forum to debate the issue first. They used conventional methods -- research, discussion, evidence -- to investigate the claim.
Although the flyer was designed to provoke an immediate reaction, the students responded with foresight and patience. They did not need to dispel rumours with force; they dispelled them with facts.
That was real change. Not loud, not viral -- but lasting.
On the last day of term, Ibrahim and Nadia sat on the school rooftop, eating sambusa and watching the city stretch below. Mogadishu's skyline glowed in the late afternoon light. Somewhere in the distance, the call to Asr drifted over the buildings.
"I am proud of us," Nadia said. "We were almost part of a lie. Now we help people question what they hear and think before they act."
Ibrahim nodded. "It is easy to shout. It is harder to think. But thinking is what makes the difference."
Amal smiled. "And thinking lasts longer than shouting."
They sat in comfortable silence, feeling something that did not need a hashtag or a viral video to be real. It was quiet. It was strong. It was a kind of serenity that comes from knowing you chose truth when it would have been easier to follow the crowd.
And in that quiet, they knew: this was just the beginning.
This story uses all 33 vocabulary words from Unit 1.

grammar.title: What Are Contrast Conjunctions?
grammar.explanation: Although, while, and whereas are called contrast conjunctions. They connect two clauses (parts of a sentence) that express different or surprising ideas. They are essential tools for showing that two things can be true at the same time even when they seem to conflict.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Although, while, and whereas are called contrast conjunctions. They connect two clauses (parts of a sentence) that express different or surprising ideas. They are essential tools for showing that two things can be true at the same time even when they seem to conflict.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are contrast conjunctions? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Use Although
grammar.explanation:
Although introduces a clause that contains a surprising or unexpected fact. The main clause contains the key information. You can place the although-clause at the beginning or the end of the sentence.
Structure: Although + subject + verb, main clause. OR Main clause + although + subject + verb.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Although introduces a clause that contains a surprising or unexpected fact. The main clause contains the key information. You can place the although-clause at the beginning or the end of the sentence.
Structure: Although + subject + verb, main clause. OR Main clause + although + subject + verb.
Examples:
Although it was raining, the students played outside.
She passed the exam although she had not studied much.
Although the rumour was plausible, Ibrahim did not believe it.
The team won the match although they had fewer players.
Notice the comma when the although-clause comes first. When it comes at the end, no comma is needed.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to use although in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Use While
grammar.explanation:
While can show contrast between two things that are simultaneously true. Be careful -- "while" can also mean "during the time that," so context matters.
Structure: While + subject + verb, main clause.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
While can show contrast between two things that are simultaneously true. Be careful -- "while" can also mean "during the time that," so context matters.
Structure: While + subject + verb, main clause.
Examples:
While Ibrahim preferred careful thinking, Nadia wanted to act immediately.
While some students enjoyed the debate, others found it challenging.
While reading is a quiet activity, debating is loud and energetic.
While the city was modern, many traditional customs remained.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to use while in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Use Whereas
grammar.explanation:
Whereas is more formal and draws a direct comparison between two different subjects or situations. It usually appears in the middle of the sentence.
Structure: Clause 1, whereas clause 2.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Whereas is more formal and draws a direct comparison between two different subjects or situations. It usually appears in the middle of the sentence.
Structure: Clause 1, whereas clause 2.
Examples:
Mogadishu is a coastal city, whereas Hargeisa is inland.
Ibrahim was cautious, whereas Nadia was bold.
Traditional news is often verified, whereas social media posts may not be checked.
Some students showed zeal, whereas others were indifferent.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to use whereas in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
grammar.explanation:
Forgetting the comma: When although or while begins the sentence, always place a comma before the main clause. Wrong: Although it was late we stayed up. Correct: Although it was late, we stayed up.
Using although and but together: Do not combine them. Wrong: Although it was cold, but we went out. Correct: Although it was cold, we went out.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Forgetting the comma: When although or while begins the sentence, always place a comma before the main clause. Wrong: Although it was late we stayed up. Correct: Although it was late, we stayed up.
Using although and but together: Do not combine them. Wrong: Although it was cold, but we went out. Correct: Although it was cold, we went out.
Confusing while (contrast) with while (time): "While I was eating" means during the time. "While I like chocolate" means in contrast. Think about whether the sentence shows a time or a difference.
Using whereas at the start of a sentence: Whereas usually works best in the middle. Unusual: Whereas I like maths, my sister likes art. Better: I like maths, whereas my sister likes art.
Memory Tip
💡 Quick Memory Trick
Although = a door to a surprise (something unexpected follows).
While = two paths running side by side (two different things at the same time).
Whereas = a spotlight on differences (directly comparing two things).
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to avoid them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Practice Exercises
grammar.explanation:
Exercise A: Fill in the blank with although, while, or whereas.
__________ the exam was easy, many students still made mistakes.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Exercise A: Fill in the blank with although, while, or whereas.
__________ the exam was easy, many students still made mistakes.
__________ I enjoy football, my friend prefers basketball.
Cats are independent, __________ dogs need more attention.
__________ it was very noisy outside, she managed to concentrate.
He speaks three languages, __________ his brother speaks only one.
__________ both teams played well, only one could win.
Exercise B: Combine each pair of sentences using the word in brackets.
It was a holiday. The shops were closed. (although -- hint: something unexpected)
Samira likes Science. Ishmael prefers English. (whereas)
The weather was hot. The students wore jackets. (although)
Some people trust social media. Others prefer newspapers. (while)
Answer Key
Exercise A: 1. Although  2. While  3. whereas  4. Although  5. whereas  6. Although/While
Exercise B: 1. Although it was a holiday, the shops were closed.  2. Samira likes Science, whereas Ishmael prefers English.  3. Although the weather was hot, the students wore jackets.  4. While some people trust social media, others prefer newspapers.
Topic 2: -ing Forms as Subjects and Objects
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply practice exercises in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Contrast Debate
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one of the following topics and prepare a two-minute speech in which you use at least two contrast conjunctions (although, while, whereas):
Social media is helpful for learning, although it can also spread false information.
Traditional news sources are reliable, whereas online posts are often unverified.
Spontaneous protests can raise awareness, while planned actions are usually more effective.
Structure your speech like this: Opening statement (state your position), Two supporting points (use contrast conjunctions to acknowledge the other side), Conclusion (restate your view with a strong closing sentence). Practise with your AI tutor or a classmate.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise contrast debate and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Retelling in Reported Speech
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a scene from the story and retell it using reported speech. For example, instead of quoting Nadia directly, say: "Nadia told Ibrahim that there was going to be a protest. She explained that her cousin had sent a flyer."
Practise retelling at least three scenes. Focus on changing the tense and pronouns correctly. This is an excellent way to internalise reported speech before you use it in writing.
Activity 3: Vocabulary Challenge
Take five vocabulary words from the unit list and use each one in a spoken sentence that relates to your own life, your school, or your community. Record yourself or practise with a partner. After each sentence, explain what the word means in your own words.
For example: "My grandmother showed great foresight when she saved money for our education, even before I was born. Foresight means the ability to think about and plan for the future."
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise retelling in reported speech and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Discussion Circle
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
If you are working with classmates or during your teacher session, discuss the following questions. Remember to use full sentences, support your ideas with reasons, and listen respectfully when others speak:
Is it ever right to join a protest if you are not completely sure about the facts? Why or why not?
How can young people tell the difference between real news and misinformation?
The story shows that silence can sometimes be a strength. Do you agree? Give an example.
What responsibilities do social media users have when they share information online?
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that social media does more harm than good.
Listen to my retelling and tell me if I used reported speech correctly.
Give me feedback on my pronunciation of these five vocabulary words.
Writing
Writing is where you bring together everything you have learned -- vocabulary, grammar, and ideas -- into a finished piece. This section guides you through structured writing tasks that build from simple sentences to a full paragraph or short essay.
Warm-Up: Sentence Building
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise discussion circle and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Six-Minute Unit Argument
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Plan and record a six-minute argument using a focused opening, connected claims with synthesized evidence, explicit source evaluation, a counterargument, rebuttal, and purposeful closing.
Develop this into a sustained five- to six-minute response with synthesized evidence, source evaluation, a counterargument and answers to follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise six-minute unit argument and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Before you submit or share any piece of writing, check it against this list:
Have I used at least two contrast conjunctions correctly?
Have I included vocabulary words from the unit list?
Is my grammar accurate (verb tenses, subject-verb agreement)?
Have I used commas correctly with although and while clauses?
Is my writing organised with a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Have I proofread for spelling mistakes?
Does my writing address the topic directly and stay focused?
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my paragraph for grammar mistakes?
I finished my essay. Can you give me feedback on how well I used contrast conjunctions?
Can you suggest better vocabulary words I could use in my writing?

activities.instructionsAndItems:
If you are working with classmates or during your teacher session, discuss the following questions. Remember to use full sentences, support your ideas with reasons, and listen respectfully when others speak:
Is it ever right to join a protest if you are not completely sure about the facts? Why or why not?
How can young people tell the difference between real news and misinformation?
The story shows that silence can sometimes be a strength. Do you agree? Give an example.
What responsibilities do social media users have when they share information online?
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that social media does more harm than good.
Listen to my retelling and tell me if I used reported speech correctly.
Give me feedback on my pronunciation of these five vocabulary words.
Writing
Writing is where you bring together everything you have learned -- vocabulary, grammar, and ideas -- into a finished piece. This section guides you through structured writing tasks that build from simple sentences to a full paragraph or short essay.
Warm-Up: Sentence Building
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

quizzes.question: What does 'concise' mean?
quizzes.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | Using few words to say something clearly.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Using few words to say something clearly.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.

quizzes.question: What does 'barricade' mean?
quizzes.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing.
quizzes.explanation: The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.

quizzes.question: What does 'zeal' mean?
quizzes.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness. | To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose. | To make someone familiar with something through repeated experience.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness.
quizzes.explanation: She studied with great zeal because she wanted the top grade.

quizzes.question: What does 'longevity' mean?
quizzes.options: A small argument about something unimportant. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose. | To make someone familiar with something through repeated experience.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts.
quizzes.explanation: Eating healthy food can improve your longevity.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Year 8 - Unit 1 Story, part 2': Several boys had built a _____ out of fruit crates and old tyres, blocking the road entirely.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Several boys had built a barricade out of fruit crates and old tyres, blocking the road entirely.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Languages and Communication: source text 1': Identify and use abstract nouns such as serenity, foresight, dissent, and _____ in appropriate contexts.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Identify and use abstract nouns such as serenity, foresight, dissent, and zeal in appropriate contexts.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Languages and Communication: source text 2': Read the _____ in your Vocabulary file and make sure you understand it in your own words.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Read the definition in your Vocabulary file and make sure you understand it in your own words.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Languages and Communication: source text 1': Verbs: _____, accustom, assimilate, concede, dispel, cite These verbs describe mental and social actions.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Verbs: allot, accustom, assimilate, concede, dispel, cite These verbs describe mental and social actions.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Languages and Communication: source text 1': For example, to _____ means to absorb and understand new information, and to cite means to refer to a source of evidence.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: For example, to assimilate means to absorb and understand new information, and to cite means to refer to a source of evidence.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Languages and Communication: source text 2': Notice how some are positive (conscientious, shrewd), some are negative (gullible, bizarre), and some are neutral (conventional, _____).
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Notice how some are positive (conscientious, shrewd), some are negative (gullible, bizarre), and some are neutral (conventional, temperate).

teacherNotes.note: Model disciplinary language aloud, teach annotation and source checking explicitly, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on evidence, cohesion, viewpoint and vocabulary precision while increasing learner independence.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Using few words to say something clearly. The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.
A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing. The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.
A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness. She studied with great zeal because she wanted the top grade.
The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. Eating healthy food can improve your longevity.
A statement that explains the meaning of a word. Look up the definition of any word you do not know.
To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose. The teacher will allot fifteen minutes for group discussion.
To make someone familiar with something through repeated experience. It took me a while to accustom myself to waking up early.
A small argument about something unimportant. They had a quibble about which film to watch but quickly agreed.
To learn and understand new information and make it part of your knowledge. When you move to a new place, it takes time to assimilate into the culture.
Having mild or moderate weather; not too hot or too cold. The temperate climate in spring is perfect for outdoor activities.
